sectionName: Experience — applied AI in regulated healthcare
intro: Five years embedded with clinical, support, regulatory and platform teams — at **Medtronic Diabetes**, and now at **MiniMed**, the diabetes-technology company spun out of Medtronic, where I'm a Senior AI Engineer leading the AI Excellence Center within the CareLink platform. The pattern repeats: an ambiguous ask, no spec, PHI everywhere, and a compliance bar most AI never clears. I take these problems end-to-end — framing, data, model, guardrails, infrastructure, deployment — until they're systems people rely on. *Details below are deliberately generalized to respect confidentiality; deeper walkthroughs available in conversation.*

orgs.name: MiniMed
orgs.meta: Senior AI Engineer · Cloud R&D · Jun 2026 →
orgs.sub: Medtronic's diabetes business, now standalone. I lead the AI Excellence Center within the CareLink platform — applied-AI product work plus the platform engineering that standardizes AI tooling and autonomous agents across teams.

cases.title: Grounded support assistant
cases.subtitle: RAG that can't invent device instructions
cases.problem: A diabetes-device companion app generates thousands of repetitive, safety-sensitive support questions. Support teams can't scale with them — and a chatbot that invents medical-device instructions isn't a UX bug, it's a patient-safety incident.
cases.shipped: A grounded RAG service on AWS Bedrock: **hybrid retrieval** (70% vector / 30% BM25) over OpenSearch with Cohere reranking (top-20 → top-5) and parent-document expansion; generation locked to approved documentation — verbatim citations, novel numbers rejected by a hallucination filter. A DynamoDB-backed semantic cache skips embed/search/rerank on similar queries (~800 ms saved); query-aware routing sends ~30% of traffic to a fast model, with extractive context compression (−34% input tokens) and prompt caching; an inline relevancy judge rejects bad context at zero extra LLM calls. PII detection, prompt-injection blocking and out-of-scope refusal guard the edges — and **every deploy is gated on a 129-question golden-set eval harness**.
cases.proof: −173 ms end-to-end P50 · −19% cost · ROUGE-L 0.556, 88.4% retrieval pass · 92% test coverage

cases.title: AI-enabled companion-app concept
cases.subtitle: one app that adapts to the whole diabetes journey
cases.problem: Diabetes apps ship one static UI for every patient — overwhelming for newcomers, limiting for advanced users. A companion-app concept had to adapt terminology, settings and AI-driven insights across the full journey, and be buildable without ever touching PHI.
cases.shipped: A stage-adaptive Flutter + FastAPI app (~26 endpoints): stage-aware terminology that swaps clinical and plain language, a settings-derivation service auto-configuring glucose targets from journey stage plus Apple Health, meal-detection ML wired into personalized insights, frictionless meal logging (search / barcode / photo), and a **stage-parameterized synthetic-data generator** so development and demos run on realistic data with zero PHI.
cases.proof: 4 journey stages · ~26 API endpoints · zero-PHI synthetic data end-to-end

cases.title: AI developer platform
cases.subtitle: AI-assistant config as versioned infrastructure
cases.problem: Every team configured AI coding assistants by hand — drifting setups, missed best practices, insecure MCP configs, secrets pasted where they shouldn't be, and slow onboarding.
cases.shipped: Declarative per-team manifests over a shared content library — **429 curated skills, rules and workflows** — applied by a one-command cross-platform installer: dry-run by default, timestamped backups, interactive secret prompting (tokens never committed), MCP server templating with per-team enable/disable, and onboarding docs auto-generated from the same source of truth.
cases.proof: 3 engineering teams · 429 curated content items · setup ~1–2 hrs → minutes (est.)

cases.title: Autonomous-agent enablement
cases.subtitle: letting an external coding agent in — without opening the doors
cases.problem: Bringing an autonomous coding agent (Devin) into the org meant exposing an internal Bitbucket Data Center to an external agent cloud — with no private-link option, and no appetite for weakening security posture.
cases.shipped: A source-IP-preserving L4 reverse-proxy architecture exposing Bitbucket over HTTPS, locked to the agent cloud's **10 static egress IPs**, with least-privilege service-account tokens, WAF rate limiting, split-horizon DNS/TLS guidance and an IT verification runbook — plus coordination of the org-wide AI-IDE license cut-over.
cases.proof: 10-IP allowlist, source IP preserved · least-privilege tokens · org-wide license cut-over

orgs.name: Medtronic
orgs.sub: Forward-deployed applied-AI engineering across the CareLink ecosystem and its data platform — AI microservices, a 15-tool release-engineering platform, PHI-safe LLM services, ML feasibility pipelines, and the regulated governance that lets AI ship in a medical-device org.

cases.title: Release-engineering automation platform
cases.subtitle: GenAI drafts, humans sign off
cases.problem: A CareLink software release means generating and cross-checking a stack of regulated documents, linking Jira tickets, and reconciling fields between a PLM system and the issue tracker by hand — slow, error-prone work where one missed inconsistency surfaces late, in an audit.
cases.shipped: Self-initiated after discovery sessions with release owners: a full-stack React + FastAPI platform exposing **15 self-service tools** — regulated document generators, GenAI-assisted drafting and ticket review through an enterprise LLM gateway (human sign-off, always), automated requirement-to-test traceability and PLM ↔ Jira consistency checks — with a background task runner streaming long jobs live over WebSockets. Dockerized, enterprise-authenticated; maintenance transitioned to the two interns and junior engineer I mentored.
cases.proof: ~80% of reg-prep automated, ~10% of prior effort · turnaround 7–8 weeks → 3–5 days · 15 tools

cases.title: Regulatory-intelligence platform
cases.subtitle: watching eight regulators so humans don't have to
cases.problem: Regulatory and quality teams monitored eight international medical-device regulators — FDA, MHRA, Health Canada, TGA and more — by hand for recalls and safety communications that carry legal weight. Slow, inconsistent, and easy to miss.
cases.shipped: A serverless pipeline scraping all **8 sources in parallel** via Step Functions (date-chunked branches → consolidate → malware check → email), an LLM relevance classifier with a **deterministic keyword override** — the model never gets the last word — site-specific extraction for the awkward sources (strict body-text matching, manufacturer-PDF letters), and a React SPA behind enterprise SSO, provisioned via CloudFormation across test, QA and prod.
cases.proof: 8 regulators → one on-demand report · manual sweep ~hours/week → on-demand (est.)

cases.title: Privacy-safe clinical LLM services
cases.subtitle: AI where PHI usually stops it
cases.problem: Clinicians drown in raw device telemetry and can't ask simple cohort questions in plain English. PHI makes most AI approaches a non-starter before they begin.
cases.shipped: Two serverless LLM services: a summarizer turning fragmented device JSON into a structured **7-section physician narrative**, and a natural-language query engine compiling plain English into a typed, validated filter DSL (operators, relative dates, JSON repair, temperature 0). Every request runs preprocess → **PHI redaction → KMS encryption → model → validated JSON**, with a sanitized audit trail behind a versioned OpenAPI contract — the compliance last mile solved first, not bolted on.
cases.proof: PHI redaction + customer-managed KMS + audit on every request · raw-JSON reading → a single glance (est.)

cases.title: Device-issue root-cause timeline
cases.subtitle: three log systems, one answer
cases.problem: To explain a single device issue, support agents cross-referenced three disjoint log systems — different schemas, dozens of event types, tribal knowledge required — and still couldn't see the sequence of events.
cases.shipped: Reverse-engineered **three undocumented device data sources** (~130 KB of exploration notes) into one model: Glue ETL consolidates warehouse unloads into PHI-aware partitioned Parquet (90-day TTL), a Lambda API behind a private gateway serves an Angular timeline with a severity fault tree, and **~70 device event types live in an editable severity catalog** with regex heuristics instead of hardcoded logic. Azure AD JWT auth and bcrypt-hashed patient IDs throughout.
cases.proof: 3 log systems → one ranked view · ~70 event types as editable config · est. ~50% faster diagnosis

cases.title: Glucose insights engine
cases.subtitle: from raw telemetry to a daily "so what"
cases.problem: Patients generate abundant glucose and insulin data but get little daily, personalized, actionable insight. The product team needed the feasibility question answered with evidence before betting a roadmap on it.
cases.shipped: An end-to-end feasibility pipeline: a warehouse SQL layer building **~12 intermediate tables** (patient qualification, a unified basal-delivery timeline), pharmacokinetic reconstruction of plasma insulin into a **72-feature vector**, a ported logistic-regression meal detector at 5-minute resolution with event grouping, and a two-pass insights engine producing per-meal glycemic metrics and device-specific 15/30-day status classifications — data lineage documented end to end.
cases.proof: 72-feature model · ~12-table documented pipeline · 4 personalized insight types

orgs.also: **Also in this era:** two FDA-committed deliverables enabling proactive sensor replacement, evaluation and enablement of 4 agentic coding tools across ~9 teams / 253 engineers (12+ workshops), LLM test-case extraction for regulated V&V (est. −70% effort), the SOP-compliant validation package that sanctioned an AI IDE for regulated use, a WAF-hardened public status page, a 22-board Flutter training prototype, a PHI-free synthetic device-data pipeline, and CareLink Pro Web localization.

orgs.name: Medtronic
orgs.sub: The classical-ML and data foundation the later AI systems stand on — predictive glucose modeling at scale, closed-loop simulation, clinical-study data engineering, and shared-platform enablement.

cases.title: Predictive glucose alerts
cases.subtitle: seeing hypo and hyper events before they happen
cases.problem: Hypo- and hyperglycemia events are preventable if you see them coming — but reactive alerts fire too late, and naive prediction drowns patients in alarms until they turn the feature off.
cases.shipped: Multi-class XGBoost models predicting hypo and hyper events **20–60 minutes ahead**, trained on **~13.6M CGM samples** with time-series feature engineering; alert packaging and post-processing for productization, and an alerts-per-day evaluation across prediction horizons to tune thresholds against alarm fatigue before anything reached a patient.
cases.proof: ~13.6M training samples · 20–60 min horizons · alarm-fatigue-aware thresholds

orgs.also: **Also in this era:** closed-loop insulin-delivery simulation with announcement-free meal detection, a patient data lake (requirements, schemas, ingestion), clinical-study data support with a digital-twin component, an XGBoost attrition model, training-compliance dashboards serving ~800 learners, and shared-AWS platform enablement for ~4 teams (~$8K/month sustained cost reduction).
